quiz_questions.question_text: A Type I error occurs when:

options.text: We fail to reject a false H0
We reject a true H0
We correctly reject H0
We correctly fail to reject H0

quiz_questions.explanation: A Type I error (false positive) occurs when we conclude there is an effect when in reality there is none.

quiz_questions.question_text: A Type II error occurs when:

options.text: We reject a true H0
We fail to reject a false H0
We correctly reject H0
The sample size is too large

quiz_questions.explanation: A Type II error (false negative) occurs when we fail to detect a real effect and incorrectly conclude there is none.

quiz_questions.question_text: The probability of a Type I error is:

options.text: Beta
Alpha (the significance level)
1 - alpha
The p-value

quiz_questions.explanation: Alpha is the probability of rejecting H0 when it is true, which is exactly the definition of a Type I error rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: The probability of a Type II error is denoted by:

options.text: Alpha
Beta
Sigma
Mu

quiz_questions.explanation: Beta represents the probability of failing to reject H0 when it is actually false (missing a real effect).

quiz_questions.question_text: Power of a test equals:

options.text: Alpha
1 - beta
1 - alpha
Beta

quiz_questions.explanation: Power = 1 - beta is the probability of correctly rejecting a false H0 (detecting a real effect when one exists).

quiz_questions.question_text: Increasing sample size will:

options.text: Increase both alpha and beta
Decrease beta and increase power (while alpha stays fixed)
Increase beta
Have no effect on error rates

quiz_questions.explanation: Larger samples make it easier to detect real effects, reducing beta (Type II error rate) and increasing power, while alpha is set by the researcher.

quiz_questions.question_text: If alpha is reduced from 0.05 to 0.01 (with sample size unchanged):

options.text: Beta decreases
Beta increases (harder to reject H0, so more likely to miss a real effect)
Beta stays the same
Power increases

quiz_questions.explanation: Lowering alpha makes the test more conservative, requiring stronger evidence to reject H0. This increases the chance of missing a real effect (higher beta).

quiz_questions.question_text: A larger true effect size makes it:

options.text: Harder to detect the effect
Easier to detect the effect (higher power)
Impossible to detect the effect
Unrelated to power

quiz_questions.explanation: Large effects produce test statistics farther from the null value, making them easier to detect and giving higher power.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a courtroom analogy, a Type I error is like:

options.text: Convicting an innocent person
Acquitting a guilty person
Correctly convicting a guilty person
Correctly acquitting an innocent person

quiz_questions.explanation: H0 is 'innocent until proven guilty.' Rejecting a true H0 (convicting an innocent person) is a Type I error (false positive).

quiz_questions.question_text: A test with 80% power means:

options.text: 80% of the time, the test will correctly detect a real effect
80% of the data is correct
Alpha is 0.80
Beta is 0.80

quiz_questions.explanation: 80% power means there is an 80% probability of rejecting H0 when it is false (and therefore beta = 0.20).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'false positive' in hypothesis testing?

options.text: Correctly identifying a real effect
Rejecting H0 when it is actually true (Type I error)
Failing to reject H0 when it is false
A positive test result from a medical screening

quiz_questions.explanation: A false positive occurs when we incorrectly conclude there is an effect (reject H0) when none actually exists. This is a Type I error.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'false negative' in hypothesis testing?

options.text: Rejecting H0 when it is true
Failing to reject H0 when it is actually false (Type II error)
Correctly failing to reject H0
A negative test result from a medical screening

quiz_questions.explanation: A false negative occurs when we fail to detect a real effect (fail to reject H0 when it is false). This is a Type II error.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'statistical power' mean?

options.text: The probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis
The probability of a Type I error
The sample size of the study
The significance level chosen for the test

quiz_questions.explanation: Power is the probability of detecting a real effect when one exists. Higher power means the test is better at finding true effects.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'beta' represent in hypothesis testing?

options.text: The significance level of the test
The probability of a Type II error (failing to reject a false H0)
The slope of a regression line
The probability of rejecting a true H0

quiz_questions.explanation: Beta is the probability of committing a Type II error, meaning the test fails to detect a real effect. Power = 1 - beta.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'effect size'?

options.text: The sample size needed for a study
The magnitude of the difference between the true parameter and the null hypothesis value
The p-value of the test
The width of the confidence interval

quiz_questions.explanation: Effect size measures how large the true difference or effect is. Larger effects are easier to detect and require smaller samples for adequate power.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'alpha-beta trade-off' mean?

options.text: Alpha and beta are always equal
For a fixed sample size, decreasing alpha (Type I error rate) increases beta (Type II error rate), and vice versa
Alpha and beta always add up to 1
Increasing alpha has no effect on beta

quiz_questions.explanation: With a fixed sample size, making the test stricter (lower alpha) reduces the chance of a false positive but increases the chance of a false negative (higher beta).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'power analysis'?

options.text: A calculation performed after the study to determine significance
A pre-study calculation to determine the sample size needed to detect a specified effect with desired power
A method for computing the p-value
A test of the electrical power needed for the study equipment

quiz_questions.explanation: A power analysis is conducted before data collection to determine the minimum sample size needed to have a good chance (usually 80%) of detecting a meaningful effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'conservative test'?

options.text: A test with a very small alpha, making it hard to reject H0
A test with a very large alpha
A test that always rejects H0
A test with no Type II errors

quiz_questions.explanation: A conservative test uses a strict (small) alpha, reducing false positives but potentially increasing false negatives. It requires very strong evidence to reject H0.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'practical significance' mean compared to 'statistical significance'?

options.text: They mean the same thing
Practical significance means the effect is large enough to matter in the real world, while statistical significance only means the effect is unlikely due to chance
Practical significance requires a larger sample than statistical significance
Practical significance is less important than statistical significance

quiz_questions.explanation: A statistically significant result may be too small to matter in practice. With large samples, even trivial differences become statistically significant.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'underpowered study' mean?

options.text: A study that uses too much electricity
A study with too small a sample size to reliably detect a real effect, resulting in high beta
A study with too many participants
A study with alpha set too high

quiz_questions.explanation: An underpowered study has insufficient sample size, leading to high probability of Type II errors and low power to detect real effects.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical screening test for a rare disease has alpha = 0.05 and beta = 0.20. In a population of 10,000 people where 50 have the disease, approximately how many false positives would you expect?

options.text: 498 (5% of the 9,950 healthy people)
50 (5% of the total population)
10 (20% of 50 sick people)

quiz_questions.explanation: False positives come from healthy people (9,950). At alpha = 0.05, about 5% of healthy people test positive: 0.05 times 9,950 = 497.5, approximately 498 false positives.

quiz_questions.question_text: A drug company tests whether a new drug lowers blood pressure more than a placebo. They set alpha = 0.05. The drug actually works (lowers blood pressure by 5 mmHg on average), but the study of only 20 patients fails to reject H0 (p = 0.12). What error has occurred?

options.text: Type I error
Type II error: they failed to detect a real effect due to insufficient sample size
No error occurred
A sampling error unrelated to hypothesis testing

quiz_questions.explanation: Since the drug actually works but the study failed to detect it, this is a Type II error. The small sample of 20 gave the test low power to detect the 5 mmHg effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: A factory's quality control test has alpha = 0.01 and power = 0.90 against a specific defect rate. If the factory produces 1,000 batches per year, about how many times per year will the test incorrectly flag a good batch as defective?

options.text: About 10 times
About 100 times
About 1 time
About 900 times

quiz_questions.explanation: With alpha = 0.01 and 1,000 good batches, we expect about 0.01 times 1,000 = 10 false alarms per year (Type I errors).

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher wants 80% power to detect a mean difference of 5 units when sigma = 15. Using a two-sided test at alpha = 0.05, she calculates she needs about 142 subjects. If she can only recruit 50 subjects, what happens to the power?

options.text: Power increases because fewer subjects is better
Power drops well below 80%, making Type II errors much more likely
Power stays at 80%
Power increases to 100%

quiz_questions.explanation: With only 50 subjects instead of 142, the test is severely underpowered. The standard error is much larger, making it harder to detect the 5-unit difference, and beta increases substantially.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pregnancy test has a false positive rate of 1% and a false negative rate of 5%. A woman who is not pregnant takes the test. What is the probability she gets a positive result?
quiz_questions.explanation: For someone who is not pregnant, a false positive is a Type I error with rate 1%. There is a 1% chance the test incorrectly shows positive.

quiz_questions.question_text: An environmental agency tests water quality at 100 different sites, each at alpha = 0.05. All sites actually have safe water. How many false alarms (significant results) would you expect?

options.text: 0, because the water is safe
About 5 (5% of 100)
About 50
Exactly 100

quiz_questions.explanation: Even when all null hypotheses are true, we expect about 5% times 100 = 5 false positives. This is the multiple testing problem.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company can choose alpha = 0.05 or alpha = 0.001 for approving a new cancer drug. The consequences of a Type I error (approving an ineffective drug) include patients receiving useless treatment. The consequences of a Type II error (rejecting an effective drug) include patients missing a potentially life-saving treatment. Which alpha should they choose?

options.text: Alpha = 0.05, because the consequences of a Type II error (missing a life-saving treatment) are severe, so higher power is important
Alpha = 0.001, because we must be absolutely certain before approving any drug
It does not matter which alpha is chosen
Alpha = 0.50 to maximize power

quiz_questions.explanation: When the cost of missing a real effect (Type II) is high (patients die without treatment), a more lenient alpha increases power. The choice depends on weighing the consequences of both error types.

quiz_questions.question_text: A study with n = 500 finds a statistically significant difference (p = 0.03) showing that a new diet reduces weight by an average of 0.3 pounds over 6 months compared to a control. Is this result practically significant?

options.text: Yes, because p = 0.03 is below 0.05
No, a 0.3-pound difference over 6 months is too small to be practically meaningful despite being statistically significant
Yes, any significant result is also practically important
No, because the sample size is too large

quiz_questions.explanation: The large sample made even a tiny 0.3-pound difference statistically detectable, but losing 0.3 pounds in 6 months has no practical health benefit. Statistical significance does not equal practical importance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spam filter is designed to catch spam emails (H0: email is not spam). If the filter has a 2% false positive rate and a 10% false negative rate, and you receive 200 legitimate emails and 50 spam emails per day, how many legitimate emails will be incorrectly sent to spam?

options.text: 4 legitimate emails
20 legitimate emails
5 spam emails
10 legitimate emails

quiz_questions.explanation: False positives (Type I errors) apply to legitimate emails: 0.02 times 200 = 4 legitimate emails incorrectly flagged as spam per day.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two researchers study the same drug. Researcher A uses n = 50 and finds p = 0.08 (not significant at 0.05). Researcher B uses n = 500 and finds p = 0.002 (highly significant). Both estimate the drug reduces symptoms by about 3%. How can both results be accurate?

options.text: One researcher must have made an error
Both are consistent: Researcher A's small sample lacked power to detect the small 3% effect, while Researcher B's larger sample had sufficient power
Researcher B's result is wrong because significant results from large samples are unreliable
The drug does not work because Researcher A's study was not significant

quiz_questions.explanation: Both studies found the same effect size (3%), but Researcher A's study was underpowered (high beta) and committed a Type II error. Researcher B had sufficient power to detect the small effect.
